family: Books: A Song of Ice and Fire
Game: A Song of Ice & Fire – Tabletop Miniatures Game

boardgame: A Song of Ice & Fire: Tabletop Miniatures Game – Stone Crows
description:
The Stone Crows are one of the many hill tribes that live across Westeros. There is not much that makes them stand, save for their loyalty to Tyrion Lannister(‘s coin). Though, even when it might be prudent to turn on him, they remained at his side.

The Stone Crows unit box for the A Song of Ice and Fire: Tabletop Miniatures Game gives Neutral and House Lannister commanders a new melee-centric combat option. Their brutality in combat knows no bounds, and while their loyalty can be easily shaken, they also readily recruit replacements for any that may stray.

Contents:
12 Miniatures
2 Unit Cards per Language (EN, PL, DE, FR, ES, HU)
1 Movement Tray
family: Books: A Song of Ice and Fire
Game: A Song of Ice & Fire – Tabletop Miniatures Game

boardgame: A Song of Ice & Fire: Tabletop Miniatures Game – Giant Spear Throwers
description:
Facing off against giants can be bad enough when they’re up-close and personal. So, you might think fighting them from afar is best. That is, until those giants start hurling spears the size of ballista bolts at you. That’s what the Free Folk are now bringing to battle.

The Giant Spear Thrower box for the A Song of Ice and Fire: Tabletop Miniatures Game gives Free Folk players walking siege engines for their force. These giants fling their spears anywhere they’re needed, even if they’re not always the most accurate. And armor does little when facing those projectiles.

Contents:
2 Miniatures (50mm Bases)
2 Cards
2 Movement Trays
family: Books: A Song of Ice and Fire
Game: A Song of Ice & Fire – Tabletop Miniatures Game

boardgame: A Song of Ice & Fire: Tabletop Miniatures Game – Umber Ravagers
description:
House Umber is more known for its berserkers. And what cavalry it has is also berserkers. The House Umber Ravagers are lightly-armored troops armed with executioner’s axes. And they know how to use them.

The House Umber Ravagers Unit Box for the A Song of Ice and Fire: Tabletop Miniatures Game gives House Stark commanders a new cavalry option. They are light and fast, striking at foes from the flanks or sides. And they get stronger when fighting outnumbered. Foes will wonder if even attacking them is a good idea.

Contents:
4 Miniatures (50mm Bases)
1 Card
1 Movement Tray
family: Books: A Song of Ice and Fire
Game: A Song of Ice & Fire – Tabletop Miniatures Game

boardgame: A Song of Ice & Fire: Tabletop Miniatures Game – Stony Shore Pillagers
description:
Settlements along the Stony Shore are highly aware of the Kraken banner coming over the horizon from sea. It means they’ll soon be visited by Stony Shore Pillagers, a nasty group of individuals ready to grab anything not nailed down, killing as they please in the process.

The Stony Shore Pillagers unit box for the A Song of Ice and Fire Tabletop Miniatures Game gives House Greyjoy players a light melee-centric unit for their forces. These troops are adept at grabbing anything worthwhile. That can entice their fallen brethren to carry on fighting long after other troops would have left.

Contents:
13 Miniatures
  ‣ 1 Sunset Sea Brave
  ‣ 12 Stony Shore Pillagers
1 Unit Card
1 Attachment Card
1 Movement Tray
family: Books: A Song of Ice and Fire

boardgame: A Song of Ice & Fire: Tabletop Miniatures Game – Baratheon Halberdiers
description:
House Baratheon is well-known for its use of heavy armor out on the battlefield. However, cavalry is known to be an effective weapon against it. That’s why House Baratheon has developed their units of Halberdiers. They’re adept at drawing enemy attacks, letting their heavier brethren march unopposed in battle.

The Baratheon Halberdiers unit box for the A Song of Ice and Fire: Tabletop Miniatures Game gives House Baratheon players, regardless of allegiance, a defensive melee unit for their forces. The Halberdiers are ideal for drawing in enemy units, sewing discord when they are ignored.

Contents:
12 Miniatures (30mm Bases)
  ‣ 1 Standard Bearer
  ‣ 11 Halberdiers
1 Unit Card
1 Movement Tray
family: Books: A Song of Ice and Fire
Game: A Song of Ice & Fire – Tabletop Miniatures Game

description:
Turtles is a two-player abstract game of sea turtles hatchlings finding their way to safety in the sea. Each player leads their turtles in opposite directions across the board, and the two opposing turtles will collide—forming and disbanding stacks of turtles, pushing each other astray, and supporting each other to safety. It's a heady yet casual game that can be delightfully surprising.

Each player starts with five turtles labeled 1-5, representing the turtles' size, strength, and weight. The goal of the game is to be the first player to get three turtles across the board and past the opposite end. This will require stacking, pushing, and careful maneuvering of the turtles on the board.

Players alternate turns, moving one turtle per turn. When a turtle moves, it may interact with other turtles based on its attributes: smaller turtles use their size to climb on the backs of others and stack up; larger turtles use their strength to push others around (or off!) the board. Stacked turtles move more efficiently, but they can be dislodged when turtles climb into them, replacing their position in the stack. And if a stack gets too large, its bottom turtles may be immobilized due to the heavy weight above them. These are some of the strategic choices players will make as they bring their turtles closer to the end and prevent their opponent from doing the same.

Note that this game requires a light amount of addition involving low numbers (1-5). And while the game mechanics may sound simple at first, it will probably take a few plays before they start to sink in.

—description from designer

Twilight is when the yokai come out to play… The winner is the person who is able to hold the Yokai Hyakkiyako (“Night Procession of 100 Yokai”).

Players draw one card each turn, choosing one of the two cards in their hand to add to their tableau and applying its ability.
•The person who collects all 8 kinds of cards wins!
•The ability of any Yokai is triggered when it is played from hand.
•Easy but deep gameplay, even for beginners!

-description from publisher

boardgame: Host Your Own Escape Room: Island Edition
description:
This interactive party game will transport you and your team to a mysterious island with a volcano about to erupt. In 90 minutes, can you find Earth’s last colony of bees and save mankind from extinction?

CONTENTS - 1x Instruction booklet, 12x Game Puzzles, 8x Tracing Paper Sheets, 1x Notepad, 1x Interactive Ending and 1x Photobooth Frame. A smartphone, tablet, or watch is required for the interactive elements

boardgame: Kingdom Death: Monster – Gilded Greatcleaver Promo Cards
description:
This is a set of two gear cards - the Gilded Greatcleaver and the Serrated Greatclever. These were included as promo cards with the 
Golden Bat Hunter - Foltron miniature, which itself is not part of the game.

The Gilded Greatcleaver can only be used for a single battle. After that, the gold fades, and the Golden Greatcleaver becomes the Serrated Greatcleaver.

boardgame: Kingdom Death: Monster – Survivors of Death 1
description: A celebration of survivors from Kingdom Death: Monster. Featuring four updated sculpts of strangers struggling to survive the darkness. This set includes a range of rules and tools representing the ways survivors might harness the world around them for their own gain. From indomitable resources to carve from the toughest monsters, to seed patterns of inspired design, to the knowledges of the arc survivors, this box celebrates all the advantages to be found in this meager existence.

Legendary characters are born with a story to tell. Included character cards foretell a journey on a grand scale. Use only one legendary character card per campaign. This character's story unfolds throughout their lifetime.

Glow uniquely designed box includes:
4 x Glow Legendary Character plastic miniatures with differing armor sets
1 x Hard plastic Kingdom Death 30mm round insert and base
Large Matte Art Print
Character Art Card
Install guide
Legendary Character card
3 x Pattern cards
3 x Pattern gear cards

Glow was born with glamorous future. Between fantasy and flesh a vast chasm of questions. A precocious child, she was a clever, nimble-fingered artificer. Piece by piece she would manifest the heart-aching enchantress glittering bright in the darkness.

Something unnatural is happening in Doria. Creatures are changing. They are getting faster. Stronger. Even more cruel than before. The trail leads to a dark alchemist whose experiments defy the laws of life. Find him before his dark creations consume the realm. And then there is an old man seeking vengeance for his lost family. His village lies in ruins, scorched by monsters that now pillage the land. Will you take his gold and help him?

Play through one of 5 additional standalone quests, each playable on its own or combined into a short story arc. Alternatively, embark on one of 2 new campaigns, each featuring 8+ scenarios.

This expansion for Dungeons of Doria introduces 8 new rooms including their unique Room Events, over 40 new Loot Cards, and 5 new opponents with distinct abilities. Plus 4 additional Characters, 20 twisted new Monster Modifiers, as well as 6 new Player Boards for tracking Hit Points and Psi Points.

Expansion content:
1 Book of Additional Rules, 1 Single Scenarios Book, 2 Campaign Books, 142 Cards (Loot, Monsters, Characters, Rules), 8 Room Tiles, 4 Character Mats, 6 Player Boards, 12 Plastic Cubes, 12 Plastic Bases, 12 Standees, 50 Tokens

—description and content from back of the box
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

boardgame: L'Ordre De Veiel: Deck Building Tactique – Les Membres De l'Ordre
description:
This expansion adds a plethora of content to the basic package, bringing some of the Order's valiant heroes to the fore. Six new heroes, each with their own unique abilities and play style, are ready to join your arsenal. Take on two fearsome new bosses that will push you to your limits.

Explore exciting new gameplay mechanics as new enemies stand between you and victory. With a variety of new additions - unique reward maps, battlefields, heroes, bosses, monsters - ‘Members of the Order’ is an excellent expansion to ODV: DBT. Explore the power and grandeur of the Order of Veiel as you push the boundaries of your tactical adventure.

Expedition: Beyond is a solo RPG card game that beckons players into a realm of untold adventures and perilous exploration. The heart of the game lies in the creation and progression of individual characters, represented by Character Cards. These cards encapsulate vital attributes such as Body, Mind, and Spirit, shaping the character's resilience, willpower, and spiritual fortitude.

The core game mechanic revolves around countering Threats in encounters by making plays with cards, and Glyphs, such as Action, Cunning, Fight, Lore, Magic, and Navigate, determine the success. Characters progress through levels, gaining attributes and Gem Play Cards as they do so. The dynamic attribute system influences a character's hand size, token limits, and special abilities.

Encounters, essential to the narrative, are varied and include combat scenarios, puzzles, and challenges. Each encounter consists of Threats, which require the matching of Glyphs to overcome, and Perils, representing negative conditions that affect characters if Glyphs aren't matched.

Strategic planning, thoughtful card selection during character creation, and effective hand management become pivotal for success. Expeditions, settings, and encounters add layers to the narrative, while the carefully crafted rule set ensures a nuanced and immersive gaming experience. "Expedition: Beyond" unfolds as an intricate tapestry of storytelling, strategy, and risk-taking, inviting players to embark on a solo journey into uncharted territories where the outcome hangs in the balance of every well-played card.

—description from the designer

With this promo pack, we not only want to add a new character and two locations to the world of Noctenburg, but also pay tribute to all those who make board games with us: designers, developers, artists, playtesters, manufacturers and everyone in the industry - we dedicate this little package to you!

This promo pack contains:

1 new Witch tile (Kendra)
2 new Building tiles (Artists' Workshop, Gamblers' Club)

—description from the designer

Add the allure of roses to your Château! Send your artisans to the rose market, complete rose card objectives, and attract new visitors to your blossoming garden.

—description from the publisher

Discover the 18th century's fascination with eastern Asian design!

Two new locations, new visitors, and vase cards to fill with exotic flowers bring new opportunities for prestige in La Fleur.

—description from the publisher

In this in-hand, solitaire dungeon crawler, you will go through the deck using and/or upgrading cards to gain skills and resources for defeating the final enemy. A different hero will be leading the fellowship on each round, adding his/her unique ability to the team

This game was inspired in part by Palm Island, an all-time favorite of mine.

—description from the designer

NINE is a simple card game in which you have to get rid of your cards to be the first to have less than 9 points.

The game is made up of 54 cards numbered from 0 to 13. There are 4 cards of each value and 2 cards of value 0.

During his turn, the player chooses doubles, triples or quadruples from his hand or a series of 5 cards to get rid of.

When one of the players has a sum equal to or less than 9 in their hand, they declare “NINE” at the time of their turn and before playing.

A chaotic zombie apocalypse-themed party card game for 2-6 players full of strategy, luck, and sabotage! Fend off zombies to stay alive, or become one and attack your friends.

You start the game as an alive player with four "Survival" cards and two "Zombie" cards (you are not allowed to look at the "Zombie" cards unless you are a zombie!) On your turn, you can play multiple "Survival" cards to sabotage other alive players or give yourself a leg up. At the end of your turn you must draw from the "Survival Deck," but beware of the zombies (the "Survival Deck" is riddled with them!) Use weapons and cures that you have collected to stay alive, otherwise you become a zombie and instead of playing "Survival" cards, you get to use the chaotic "Zombie Deck."

As a zombie, you can play multiple "Zombie" cards to attack alive players to make them zombies or in an attempt to get cured by them. You will end your turn by drawing from the "Zombie Deck."

The last player alive wins! An alternate ending (also described in the rulebook) allows for a zombie victory (and a much greater test of survival)-- The last player alive must fend off a final zombie attack to win!

-description from publisher

Hello Kitty's tea time is coming! Sanrio characters bring their favorite desserts and drinks to the party and try to earn Hello Kitty's favor.

Hello! Tea Time is a re-implementation of Vegetable Stock and is re-themed with Sanrio Characters. It's only available in Taiwan.
There are some changes in the rules:
1. Instead of multiplying the number of vegetables and their prices at the end of the game, players try to have the majority of each type of dessert to score.
2. During scoring, the desserts provide different amounts of VPs based on their popularity levels.
3. The game is played over 2 rounds, where the second round provides a higher overall VP.
4. The popularity levels of each dessert at the end of the first round will be the starting points for the second round.
5. Each player has a secret "favorite" dessert, adding 1 more icon to compete with the majority.

boardgame: Summoner Wars (Second Edition): Veldimax the Great Promo Card
description:
Veldimax the Great is a mercenary champion who will provide his summoner with a magic point every time he attacks and rolls specials, but if you don't keep him occupied with attacks he'll take off right away.

Initially sent out with subscriber packages of the Deepwood Groaks and The Forged faction packs in January 2024, then handed out at Gen Con in August 2024.

boardgame: IADS: The Game Advanced Capabilities Expansion Set
description: This Expansion to "IADS: The Game" adds to the depth and complexity of the original. This set allows up to six players, adds to the number of core cards, and adds more weapon systems to the IADS inventory.

A puzzling tile game from a fantasy culture. Each player removes four tiles from their set of 36 and competes against the other to be the first to complete 2 hexagonal constructions. The game is set within the context of a fantasy world and comes with a description of the development of various games and entertainments within that culture that utilise the tiles. Players are encouraged to utilise the game with their own RPG or LARP setting. From the descriptions given it may be inferred that there is more than meets the eye to the tiles and they may hide deeper mysteries.

—description from the designer

Making Honey Hunny is an educational and interactive ​game designed to teach players about the intricate ​process of honey production while racing to reach a ​delicious pancake breakfast. Players navigate through ​various stages, from bees collecting nectar to ​beekeepers harvesting the honey. Along the way, ​players encounter boosts and setbacks that make the ​play more unpredictable. The ultimate goal is to be the ​first to complete all stages and arrive at the pancake ​breakfast, where they can savor the sweet reward of ​their learning adventure.

boardgame: Ordre de Veiel: Deckbuilding Tactique – Extension 2: Les arts interdits
description: Discover The Forbidden Arts, a fascinating expansion that explores the dark and powerful mysteries of the occult. Immerse yourself in a world of forbidden magic and taboo powers as six new heroes and two fearsome bosses join the adventure. ‘Forbidden Arts’ brings innovative card effects that manipulate corpses and exile. Explore these mysterious powers, face devious adversaries and discover how to harness the darkest forces.

boardgame: Ordre de Veiel: Deckbuilding Tactique – Extension 3: Les corrompus
description: In this new expansion, two new heroes join the ranks to face an all-new boss who embodies corruption itself. The expansion brings a new dimension of play with 'Essences of Corruption' – devastating cards that inject destructive power into your arsenal. However, corruption is not without consequences. With ‘Afflictions’, players experience the effects of this cursed power, adding a layer of complexity to every decision. Explore the dark sides of corruption!

You are a corsair commanding a vessel navigating the oceans. The die tells you where to move around the island and when to land on it. The die can also activate or deactivate the Letter of Mark allowing you to double the coins you can win. You can attack the other players and fight for more coins.

The game continues until all the Treasure Island coins have been distributed to the players. The winner is whoever possesses the most coins at the end of the game.

There are no limits on the number of players or playing time, both can be increased or decreased as desired, as long as additional playsets and coins are available.

Expansions
The game sets are distinguished by the cardinal points, (N, E, S, W) and (NE, SE, SW, NW). Each set can be played by 2 to 7 players. By combining 2 game sets, the number of players can be increased to 14 vessels.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Ordre de Veiel: Deckbuilding Tactique – Extension 4: Divine corruption
description: Inspired by the universe of our novel, this extension is an opportunity to highlight some of the protagonists of ‘Divine Corruption’. Taken from the pages of our history, four new heroes join the adventure to face four new bosses who embody the most formidable challenges the game has to offer.

graph-it is an math educational board game for players to draw and map out different linear equations (sometimes some reciprocals). There's two variations of play for the game.

In the standard version, players take turns drawing equation cards to map out all the possible coordinate points for that equation on the board. If the opponent notices they missed any, they can claim that spot with their chip.
If another player's chip is a part of your set of solutions, you can claim that chip by flipping it.
The player who has the most chips when the deck runs out wins the game!

In the solving variation, players instead draw tables of coordinate points. They map out the table, and the opposing player is supposed to derive the equation from the board. If they get it right, then the opponent wins the card. If they get it wrong, the drawer gets to keep the card.
The player who has the most cards when the deck runs out wins the game!

—user summary

Players take turns spinning either spinner to land on a choice of two different products (result of multiplication). The player can choose either one, then they must place their chips onto the board for every unique combination of factors for that number.
For example, if 12 is picked, the player can play on the coordinates (3,4) and (1,6), but cannot play on (4,3) or (6,1) because those numbers were used up already.

If the opponent notices a coordinate the player missed, he may claim it for themself.

The first player to get 3 in a row vertically or horizontally wins the game!

A variation of the game includes changing the winning condition to have 3 chips  in a row diagonally.

—user summary

Starfire is a game of battles between fleets of space warships. Each player commands a fleet of starships, moves their starships, and fires their weapons.

This is a 2014 PDF version of the rules (and only the rules, not any of the other physical components) that were originally published in 1984 for the second edition of the Starfire board game. This edition of Starfire is a combination of the original Starfire and Starfire II with rules editing and corrections; for example several rules changes were made to fighters. As such, 2nd Edition Starfire contains the tactical combat rules for both starship and fighter combat in a single book. Furthermore, it includes 19 scenarios that introduces the first interstellar conflict between the Terran Federation and the Khanate of Orion, and the third interstellar conflict between the Terrans, Khanate, and the Rigelians.

The Dreamt have been watching, waiting for their moment to help a select few, the chosen, create a bond strong enough to leave all they’ve ever known behind...Escaping Reality for another life in the Dream World.

Escaping Reality is a competitive simultaneous worker placement resource management with an optional combat dice rolling mechanism. You play as The Dreamt, Avatars with unique asymmetric abilities, competing to become the most powerful entity in the Dream World. The goal of Escaping Reality is to be the player with the most victory points at the end of the game. Victory points are gained primarily by gathering enough comfort (resources) to help people fall asleep…Escaping Reality and entering the Dream World forever; however, this is not the only path to victory. Players can befriend companions, acquire unique artifacts, fulfill the wildest of dreams, conquer Realms, and defeat the worst of Nightmares, all of which also provide victory points.

—description from the designer

Welcome to Camp Macabre… If you’re not careful, your sleeping bag will become your body bag!

Inspired by a love of ’80’s horror movies, Camp Macabre, the Horror Survival Card Game, pits 2 – 7 players in a bloody competition to be the player with the last Camper standing.

At Camp Macabre you’ll control Campers pitted in an epic struggle for survival against menacing hordes of Monsters, while at the same time unleashing your own Monsters to destroy your opponent’s Campers. When you control the sole surviving group of Campers, you win the game!

—description from the publisher
publishers: Knights of the Round Hill Game Design Inc.

boardgame: Grave Keeper: A Victorian Roll & Write Game
description:
Grave Keeper is a print and play, roll and write game for 1+, in which players take on the roll of a Victorian grave keeper managing the crypts of four noble families.

Bury the dead, maintain graves, lead funeral processions and fend off grave robbers! Will you earn the highest reputation and be the best grave keeper in town?

Driven by 4 coloured D6’s and a pencil, the game plays over 6 rounds and takes about half an hour.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Flesh and Blood: Dusk til Dawn
description:
Dusk till Dawn is an expansion booster set that marks the second chapter of the epic conflict between Light and Shadow, and sees the return of fan favourite Light Illusionist and Shadow Runeblade classes, whose predecessors have attained Living Legend status.

Light Warrior and Shadow Brute also unlock new abilities as the struggle between the forces of Solana and the Demonastery approach a thrilling chokepoint.

As the 10th booster set, Dusk till Dawn marks a special milestone for Flesh and Blood. To celebrate, it contains a collection of treasures to eclipse anything that has come before.

The Empire is shattered and great cities struggle for dwindling resources in this high tactics card game for 2 players. Send your characters to battle with support of spells and gear. Each use pushes them closer to exhaustion, leaving them vulnerable to defeat. Our first set of 235 cards introduces 15 cities, each home to 2 legendary heroes.

Soulshards: Broken Alliance ECG is a high tactics expandable card game for 2 players. Each player uses their own deck assembled from their favorite cards. During the game, players take on the role of a captain of the guard for a city at war with the other player's city. Each player will obtain and play cards from their deck which represents their forces and resources to defeat the opposing city. Before the game, each player creates a deck of 40 Character, Spell, and Gear cards that is paired with 1 City card. The City is your most important card. It represents the home you fight to protect. It is the domain of your forces that will fight on the battlefield, the bedrock of the magical institutions that allow you to cast spells, and the territory of massive beasts that will join you in the fight.

During the game, your deck is called your Arsenal. At any time you have no cards in your Arsenal, lose 3 Resolve, then shuffle all cards from your Home into your Arsenal, excluding any Spell currently being Cast. You may also have up to 12 cards as your Reserves deck which allows you to change your deck based on your opponent before starting the game. These cards count as part of your deck for determining how many copies of each card you can use.

Over the course of the game you'll be playing cards into different zones, the Keep, Stockpile, Arsenal and more. Some zones, like the Battlefield, you'll only be able to move placed cards into. On your turn you'll be strategically playing cards, moving cards, collecting attributes from cards in your Stockpile, and engaging with your enemy! All with the goal of causing enough damage to wipe out their Resolve! Each player begins with 12-16 Resolve as determined by their City. When your opponent has no Resolve remaining, they lose the game, and you win.

—description from the designer

Two clans, divided from the trials of being exiled, have held onto twin sacred amulets, prophesized to guide them back to their rightful kingdom. On a rare Fire Moon eclipse, both amulets have lit up, guiding them to the Boiling Sea and a mysterious monolith. Upon reaching the monolith, the light from the amulets ceased, and the monolith lit up, pointing each clan at two separate locations on the distant mountain. The two clans now race each other to build the Crimson Keep and summon the fabled dragon to rule over their newly reclaimed kingdom!

Crimson Keep is a dark fantasy, tower-building, card placement, set-collection, strategy game. To begin the game you'll draw five cards from your deck and choose 3 to keep. Each turn you'll take two actions, Place or Replace a card, Flip a Card over (to activate it) or Draw Cards, Use a Special Ability or Attack, or Move. Each of these actions has a track that you'll be moving up, which will gain you bonus actions when you reach the top of the track! Carefully manage your actions and hand, because if you end your turn with no cards in hand, you'll automatically draw up at the beginning of your turn, instead of wasting an action on drawing cards!

There are three different types of cards, Building, Combat and Cavern. When you are Placing a card, you always place it on the inactive side. Once Flipped, the card is on the active side, and you'll be able to use its special ability. When you place a building card, you'll be building up your Keep! You'll want to make sure its tall, strong and fortified! Combat cards will be how you attack the rival clan! And cavern cards will let you have enhanced actions!

The actions you take throughout the game, winning combats, building up your keep, and more will allow you to discover runes, which you will need to summon the mighty dragon! You'll need to pay attention to your Summon Board to focus on the actions that will get you the highest value runes possible! The higher the total value of runes you acquire, the more powerful dragon you'll be able to summon. But you'll need to keep a close watch on your rival's rune discovery progress to make sure they don't beat you to the punch!

Once one of the clans summons the dragon,  an epic final battle ensues! The dragon will now face off against the other clan's fortress. Have you built it well enough to withstand the dragon's fearsome attacks? Each time you repel an attack by the dragon, you will injure it! If your fortress manages to defeat the dragon, you will rule over the kingdom! But if your keep falls, your rival will take control of the land!

There is a town, unguarded, and you are intent on raiding it and leaving with a massive bounty of loot!
But be careful, there are others who share a similar interest… Play your cards right and take home the biggest stash!​

STASH is quick, simple, and easy to learn.
Designed to be endlessly re-playable by both casual partygoers and dedicated strategists, STASH will have you hooked. It has the simplicity of UNO, the anticipation of Poker, and the interaction of Unstable Unicorns.

Released by the independent Australian game developer in 2025, STASH received overwhelmingly positive feedback from play-testers, and delivers on its promise to strike the balance between a casual family favourite and a medium for competitive play.

Gameplay:
Players are villains, taking turns adding to their loot pile, accumulating treasure. Until it is stashed, however, it is at the mercy of the other players.
Players start with a hidden villain - each with their own special action. On a player's turn, they either raid the town by drawing and playing a card, or they reveal their hidden character card, allowing them to stash, steal, trade, or burn each other's loot.

The game ends when no more cards can be played from player's hands, and the highest accumulated loot score determines the winner. There are a predetermined number of each card type within the deck, allowing competitive players to strategically assess the risk vs reward of their actions.

—description from designer
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
2 games in 1! Scrabble and Scrabble Together

Experience Fun Beyond Words with the Scrabble board game, now featuring two games in one! Players can enjoy the classic Scrabble crossword game they know and love building words and angling for Double Letter and Triple Word scores or they can flip the board and try Scrabble Together, a quick, casual game where players cooperate to complete word challenges.

In Scrabble Together, work as a team to complete an assortment of 50 Goal Cards that help teach Scrabble basics.

Draw a card and reveal challenges like 'Play a five-letter word.'
Complete the challenge and take the card to earn a point super-simple scoring!

Goal Cards include 20 Beginner Goal Cards, 30 Standard Goal Cards, and 6 Helper Cards complete 20 goals to win!
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Introducing Hubble Run, a thrilling board game set in the year 11932 where players race their spaceships through the stars! Designed for 2 to 12 players, this fast-paced game combines strategy and chance, each session lasting just 30 minutes.

In Hubble Run, you’ll navigate a board filled with interwoven routes and unpredictable Hazard cards, all in real-time—there are no turns! Players continuously roll dice to move ships, overcome obstacles, and battle it out with their opponents. The first ship to cross the finish line ends the game and wins it all!

What makes Hubble Run unique is its constant action and depth of strategy. Compete in the annual ‘Hubble Run’, commemorating the Dynamic Interstellar Cab Engine (D.I.C.E.) that transformed space travel in the 12th millennium. So, gather your crew, roll your dice, and may the best pilot win!

—description from the designer

Trouble at Hand is a handheld sologame, in which you are fighting against all kinds of bullies.

You start with only having some friends and allies on your side, but as the game progresses some of them will get exhausted.
So you better try to build a good support system - and fast - you never know, when you need some extra help.
The challenges you face are getting harder and harder but each mastered challenge will turn into a special tool you need to fight the bully. 
There is no other way to win but to defeat the bully.

Trouble at hand is played in your hands only and over 12 rounds. The difficulty of the game increases during play, but so do your chances when you play it right. 
You rotate and flip the cards to up- or downgrade your cards and
the round cards inform you of any additionally change of rules during play. 
It plays in about 10-30 minutes, but you can also tuck it away at any moment utilising the provided player aids.

For each game you only need 6 friends, 6 allies, 6 challenges and 1 bully, but each box contains 36 friends and allies, 12 challenges and 6 bullies of various difficulty level.

(All cards are randomly selected and distributed to each game out of a total of 216 friends and allies, 36 challenges and 24 bullies, so no game contains the same set cards.)

The game also includes ways to modify your game to make it easier or harder.

—description from the designer
family: Series: Circle of Friends (Lonely Kid Games)

boardgame: Let's Go! To Japan: Matsuri Edition
description:
The Matsuri Edition:

     Arrives wrapped in an elegant Let's Go! To Japan designed furoshiki cloth.
     Gold foil embossed box.
     5th player components.
     Wood tokens instead of cardboard.
     3 cloth deck and token storage bags.
     5 seigaiha pattern player bits storage bags.
     Travel journal instead of scorepad.
     Includes the Day Trip expansion.
     Includes Ceramic Token Bowls.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
You play a committee member in 18th century France facing grave crises. Each crisis can be resolved through a reform but each committee member has his or her own secret objectives.

Each committee member is either a Monarchist, a Republican or a Bonaparte. To win a Revolution victory for the Monarchists or the Republicans, you must start a revolution and name all your faction members (no more, no less). If you fail, the opponent faction may start a counter-revolution. If both fail, the Bonapartes win.

The players take turns to play the following phases:


     Seat: Play a seat token next to two crises
     Vote: If all seats are occupied, play a secret vote for a reform (Monarchist/Republican).
     Purge: If the vote is not unanimous, play an open vote to purge a Committee member and have his or her seat token removed.


Revolution: Optionally start a revolution by naming your faction members.

If the game ends without a Revolution victory, committe members with fulfilled objectives win, e.g. Louis XVI win if all three reforms are Monarchist.

—description from designer

"Wild" and "Villain" cards given away at Gen Con.
Add these cards to the deck and proceed as usual.

Fall Season Promo 2024 of FryxGames.

Promo X69

Hospitals is an active card (blue) with a building tag
Cost 8MC
Effect: Each time a city is placed, gain a disease here.
Action: Remove a disease from ANY OF YOUR CARDS to gain 1 M€ per city in play.
Decrease your energy production 1 step. Gives 1VP.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Score the most points over 4 quarters and show your rivals who's boss!

Each turn, play a 5-card hand to score points and stop your rivals!  Score a touchdown by playing cards that combine to 100+ yards, score 3 points by playing a field goal card and stall your opponents with feud cards!  Each turn represents 1 quarter and play finishes after 4 turns


     Easy to learn: score touchdowns in under 5 minutes and be hooked for hours! Dive into fast-paced and competitive action that is both addictive and family friendly



     A game for all ages and places: our interactive gameplay is a hit for family game night, beach trip, road trips, camping trip or even to play during half time! Kid, teen and adult friendly. Ages 7 and up



     Choose your game plan wisely:  play the right cards at the right time to outwit your opponents! Most cards are worth a different yardage, while playing 3 of a kind gives you a bonus 100 yards and rare quarterback and running back cards double pass and rush yards!



     Bring the defensive heat: stop your opponents with feud cards to get the edge! Feud cards are rare (only 4 in the deck- interception, sack x2, fumble) and are used to sabotage your opponents' strategies. When these cards are played, player interaction and trash-talk are at their highest!


—description from the publisher

Each Anno Domini game consists of 340 cards, with a description of a historical event on one side of the card and the year (and sometimes specific date) in which it happened on the other. Like all Anno Domini sets, this game can be played as a standalone or mixed with some or even all editions.

In Anno Domini: Eco, which focuses on money and currencies, each player receives nine cards (or fewer, if you want the game to be shorter) and may look only at the descriptions. In turn, players place a card on the table, trying to place their card in chronological order to those already present. Instead of adding a card, a player may claim that the order in which the cards have been placed is incorrect. In this case all cards are turned over and the correct years revealed.

If the order is correct, then the doubting player receives two cards and skips a turn. If the order is incorrect, then the previous player – who accepted the order as correct or made it incorrect through her own placement – receives three cards. The first player with no cards remaining in hand wins.

Part of the Anno Domini series

Front Line is a 2 player game, ages 12 & up, taking place in four rounds, from September to December. Rounds have six turns, three per player. To win: Have the most Occupation Points (OP) at the end of December or occupy opponent’s Capital to immediately win the game. Ties are possible.

ON Your Turn: 1st: Take (choose) unused supply chit/s (a stack of 1-4 chits), from your Available Supply, set them above your Items card (holds units/Leadership and used supply chits), they are now available for use (Actions). Now: Only the chits you chose can be used for Actions and/or if Leadership is available, you may also do a Maneuver, Relieve or Cache Task. Rally and Opportunity Fire Tasks are only used on an opponents turn.

Use any type of supply, for these Regular Actions:

Amphibious Move (optional): Move Active unit on either Port. To an empty Port or Beach.

Attack: Active Unit targets Unfriendly unit within its’ Range. Active Unit targets Unfriendly Empty City at a Range of one. Roll for Result.

Commission: Set on right side of Items card, your exhausted Leadership (under Items card). At end of turn, set it atop your “Active Leadership” area.

Move: Active unit travels up to 2 spaces (+1 if moving through one or more deserts).

Send Support (Allies Game ): May grant, one per turn, a regular action, your Allies choice, to be used at once, as if it’s their turn.

Allies Game (2-4 Players): You will need two copies of Front Line. Set two Battleﬁeld beside each other. A player controls one or two countries. Pick who gets the 1st turn. Amphibious Move is not optional and it can now can move, an Active unit on any Port, to any empty beach/port on either battleﬁeld. If you send a unit to your Allies Battleﬁeld: it will no longer be under your control. Players with 2 Allied countries, can Send Support and Amphibious Move units to either country they controle.

Missions: Use 1-3 (Red/Blue/Green) supply, to do the Mission associated with its’ color. Built/Regrouped units, may do a 2nd action (if supplied) after mission is done. Unit doing Paratroop, may do another action (if supplied) before/after the mission. Unit doing missions, can be attacked via Opportunity Fire. Paratroop, Use 1 Air Supply: Take your active Infantry, on an Air Base, and set them up to two land spaces from a friendly City or friendly Active Unit. Build, Use 2-3 Industry Supply: Set as active, a unit from your reserve, on your countries empty Industry. 2 supply sets an Artillery/Infantry. 3 sets a Tank. Regroup, Use 1 Capital Supply: Pick one of your disrupted units. Make it Active by flipping the unit from its’ disrupted side, to its active side.

Game Ends after the December round or you win Immediately, upon entering an opponent’s Capital (“W” Hex in opponents' country). Once all six turns are completed in December, the player with the most OP (below) wins. If players tie for most OP; the player with most units (active or disrupted) wins, otherwise, game ends in a tie. Peace In Our Time!

Advice: Most ﬁghting is in the mid section of board and around the Air Bases so... try to control the entire side of the board your Air Base is on. Use your Artillery for attacking disrupted units anywhere and active units on mountains. Tanks move best when near deserts and are deadly on higher ground. Infantry on Hills and Mountains are good at blocking opposing units. Use Leadership sparingly so your opponent will be more cautious.

Leadership: When your Leadership Card is on the “Leadership Available” space, you may do one of its' ﬁve Tasks. After use, tuck it under the bottom of your Items card showing it’s Exhausted. Commission Action sets it to the right of your Items card (ﬁg 5) and when your turn ends, set it on “Leadership Available”.

A Task on your Leadership card is available if, your Leadership card is active and you fulfill all the Tasks qualiﬁcations. The Maneuver and Relive tasks can be used on any players turn. Cache can only be used on your own turn. Rally and Opportunity Fire tasks are only used on your opponents turn. Remember: A Task is not an action. It does not cost supply but it does Exhaust Leadership when used.

—description from designer

Stand or Starve: A Strategic Survival Game

Overview: 
Set in the tumultuous year of 794 AD, Stand or Starve immerses players in a historic conflict where four powerful factions—Samurai, Knights Templar, Vikings, and Spartan warriors—clash for supremacy. Each faction seeks to establish its dominance, shaping the course of history through strategic decisions, resource management, and intense combat. Will you lead your empire to victory, or will your people succumb to starvation?

Gameplay: 
Stand or Starve is a competitive strategy game where 2-4 players must balance resource management, territory expansion, and combat to outlast their opponents. Players take on the role of faction leaders, each vying to build the strongest empire while fending off rivals.

Game Phases and Player Actions:


    Setup: 


Players choose a faction and a capital city, receive initial resources (food, wood, metal, villagers, warriors, and roads), and determine the turn order.
Turn Structure: On their turn, players allocate villagers to produce resources, build roads, train warriors, and expand their empire. Players can also engage in trade with others to gain the upper hand.

    Resource Management: 


Assign villagers to gather food, wood, and metal or to train additional villagers and warriors.
Construction: Use wood and metal to build roads, towers, and resource stations, which are essential for expansion and defense.

    Combat: 


Move warriors to capture enemy capitals, stations, and roads. Battles are resolved by dice rolls, with bonuses from tools and defensive structures influencing the outcome.
Special Cards: After the second cycle, players draw event and tool cards that can alter the course of the game for everyone or provide individual advantages.

    End of Turn: 


At the end of each turn cycle, players must manage their food supply, as starvation can lead to elimination.

Winning and Losing:


    Victory Condition: 


The game ends when one player's capital remains standing after all others have fallen or been seized. This player is declared the winner.

    Losing Conditions:


Players are eliminated if their food supply runs out or if their capital is captured for a full cycle.

    Tie Condition: 


If multiple players starve simultaneously, the game ends in a tie.

Game Components:
1 Game board with dual maps
112 Playing cards
112 Wooden pieces (pawns, warriors, & rooks)
4 Dice
1 Special orange cylindrical token
1 Special orange cube
100 White road markers

description:
In this game, your objective is to acquire more legendary treasures, known as "Beast Relics," than your opponents. However, to obtain these relics, you must possess a secret map. If another player holds the map, you must move your kayak card to the same area as that player and engage in a dice battle. You can also use gadgets or previously acquired Beast Relics to your advantage in seizing the map.

Amid the chaos, the "Kikitata Tribe," guardians of the treasures, are stealthily approaching, ready to attack the players.

Kidnapping is a trick-taking where you have to NOT follow suit: A card in a suit that has already been played is a loser regardless of rank. The game is similar to the classic game Hearts.

In Kidnapping, players put baby goats (“kids”) to sleep for the afternoon (“napping”). Players fill their hands with napping cards of four kinds: pillows, harps, lassoes and baseball bats. The goats differ, and many resist a rest from different sleep aids.

Each trick, a random kid is drawn, and each player plays a card in turn. The first player wins by default, but subsequent players can win if their play is a new suit and is at least equal to the current winner.

A round (called an afternoon) consists of ten randomly-selected kids, and each player gets one point for each kid they put to bed (each trick won). Afternoons continue until at least one player passes a pre-decided number of points. If there is 1-3 players, there is an artificial player.

The game has 54 cards: 40 Napping cards and 14 Kid cards. The illustrations are from the public domain site pixabay.

WIP Page: https://boardgamegeek.com/thread/3352801/wipkidnapping-2024-54-card-game-design-contest-con

This game is  entered in the  54-card gane design contest: https://boardgamegeek.com/thread/3327975/2024-54-card-game-design-contest

In Sideral, you must place planets strategically on the board, trying to keep them close together in order to cluster them, forming larger and larger galaxies. The first player to form a level-4 galaxy wins.

Enlightened is a climbing card game inspired by traditional Chinese favorites like Big Two, Da Lao Er, and Zheng Fen. Players vie to be the first to empty their hand, but with a unique twist - there's no trump suit! Instead, you'll harness the dynamic interplay of Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, and Water.

Each element has its place in the cycle, mirroring the balance found in nature. The Wu Xing destruction cycle determines the elemental ranking: Fire melts Metal, Metal chops Wood, Wood parts Earth, Earth absorbs Water, Water extinguishes Fire.

Objective & Scoring:

The object of the game is to get rid of all the cards in your hand before any of your friends get rid of theirs. The faster you get rid of your cards, the higher your score will be. After one player has no more cards, the round ends immediately. The losing players count how many cards are left in their hands. The winner of the round receives a score equal to the sum of all other players' remaining cards. The losers of the round score 0 points.

What is Enlightenment?

In the first round of the game, there is no enlightenment. In later rounds, the player who gets rid of all their cards first will be enlightened, which means they must offer their highest card to a player of their choice. Then that player can give any card they want in return to the enlightened player.

So, how do we play this?

Players aim to empty their hands first by playing increasingly powerful combinations. Cards rank by number and element, following the Wu Xing cycle. Play single cards, pairs, three-of-a-kinds, and five-card combinations. Unique "Illumination" combos can break the cycle. The first to play all cards wins the round and scores points.

—description from the designer

Fil Rouge is a series of co-operative and narrative games by Julien and Tom Prothière. In each chapter, participants must find the thread of the story and put the cards dealt back in order in the Frieze. Although the game is designed to be played only once, it offers a deeply enriching experience that remains etched in the memories. The four chapters can be played consecutively or in several times, live the story at your own pace.

Each chapter is divided into a certain number of cards, distributed among the players, except one which is placed in the Frieze. They will be able to communicate freely, without showing each other their cards, in order to find the thread of the story. The discussions will lead the players to reveal the cards and place them in the Frieze to reconstruct the story. The intention is to listen, talk about emotions, and experience sensitive exchanges through the game and the story it offers.

In Fil Rouge: Dans Leurs Regards ("In Their Eyes"), you dive into the intimacy of Christmas Eve, with each member of the family telling you their version of the event and offering a unique perspective on the moments shared. Between laughter, tension, and memories, discover a Christmas experienced through different eyes in which each story, even the simplest, takes on a personal and touching dimension.

Welcome to our lab!
For decades now, we the scientists of Giete (Groupement Intergouvernemental d'Experts sur la Transition Énergétique), have been feeding the world governments with data on an environmental situation that has only gotten worse.

Faced with constant inaction from our heads of state and major corporations, and despite the urgency of the situation, we have allocated part of our meagre budget to the development of artificial intelligence (A.I.).

Like a digital hummingbird, this A.I. will gather the results of experiments carried out by volunteers, feeding the algorism with their trials, their behaviors and their initiatives. Little by little, it will experiment with them different social and political systems, each of which may be able to provide part of the solution. And finally, your participation will provide data on the urgent decisions that need to be made.

Each of the 16 Experiments will lead you to use the resources available in a given area to meet the population's energy needs, within a limited timeframe and under a defined system.

Happy experimenting!
A game full of Science!

The game also comes with a 68-page book ("Avant d'éteindre la lumière…") which is not a manual on energy transition, but rather a companion document to the game. Written by energy expert Pascal Lenormand, it explains why the A.I. asks players to perform abstract actions, and draws parallels with the real-life systems behind them.

Cooperative and evolutionary!
Cooperative: you'll win or lose together. Either way, you're going to gain a special human moment!
Evolutive: depending on the experiments you play, you'll need to adapt the rules and the cards you use. Be prepared: this is a game where you'll need to learn, unlearn and relearn a lot!

Your goal
Each Experience consists of 3 rounds. Round 3 features an optimized test with a special rule.  To win a game, you need to win all 3 rounds.

If you win all 16 experiments... something will happen.
On the other hand, you lose immediately at the end of a round if you fail to meet a population's energy needs or pollute more than is sustainable.

Note: The game requires a free application to be downloaded from the App Store or Google Play. Once downloaded, an internet connection is not required for gameplay.

boardgame: Uskoci: A Card Game about Croatian Pirates
description:
It is the year of our Lord 1603. The Uskok Warlord Radoslav died during the last Venetian raid. All the Uskok leaders, including you, were invited to Nehaj, the unconquerable Uskok fortress, in order to choose their next Warlord.

Objective of the Game
Be the first to capture 3 ships. As soon as one player captures their 3rd ship, the game is over (the round is not completed). A player captures a ship if the card they played brings the total resources to exactly 13.

All players simultaneously place one card from their hand face down in front of them on the table. Then, in turn, starting with the player with the advantage and continuing clockwise, they play their cards. Players reveal their cards only when it is their turn to play. The card is played on one of the three ships in the center. Cards on the ships are placed next to each other so that the numerical values are clearly visible. This sequence repeats until one of the players has fewer than 3 cards in their hand, ending the round.

Gliss is an intricate game of strategy for two players, Red and Blue. In Gliss, players place down bases, deploy fleets of flying shapes and claim enemy stones to establish new bases and control towers. Unlike similar games in this family, such as 1972’s Realm, the game uses the simplest of generic equipment, allowing it to be played easily by anyone. This is because Gliss uses an intriguing system of moving and stationary glyphs to generate all the action in the game. A game of Gliss cannot end in a draw. Corey L. Clark designed Gliss in August 2024.

Equipment: Any board of any size between 12x12 and 19x19 and an unlimited supply of stones in two colors.

Note: The game is played on the vertices of the board, as with Go, stones are only said to be adjacent if they are connected by the vertices of the board.

Definitions: In Gliss only three shapes are allowed on the board, these are:
Base: A Base is a 2x2 block of stones in one player’s color
Glider: A Glider is a bent shape consisting of 3 stones in a player's color. A Glider has a Core and Arms. A Glider can move in the two directions in which the arms project from the corner stone which is the core.
Control Tower: a Control Tower is a single stone in one player's color, obviously with no friendly stones adjacent

Gameplay: On your turn you have two options: 

     build a Base.
     Alternatively you may deploy a Glider from a base, move a Glider or Dock a Glider, or potentially all 3 of these in combination, depending on the move.


Building: you may place a Base next to nothing or enemy stones; if placed next to friendly stones this creates an illegal shape.

Deployment: Any base may deploy 1 of 4 Gliders embedded in it(one for each set of directions possible). Simply take the stones comprising the Glider and move them to a destination according to the conventions in the "Movement" section next.

Movement: A Glider may move in a series of orthogonal steps according to one or both of the directions indicated by its arms. A Glider may and must continue moving as long as it would create an illegal shape with the friendly pieces nearby by landing or it would otherwise land on top of them, the Glider however must terminate its movement as soon as it could land, either by itself or as part of a Base, at that point either landing, or else reverting the movement in the case that any enemy control tower is in the way of the landing zone.

Docking: Docking is moving a Glider so it forms a Base with either a friendly or enemy Control Tower. If you dock a Glider with an enemy Control Tower the enemy tower you can immediately convert it to your color, unless this would create an illegal shape.

Capturing: the only shapes that can be captured by replacement (as opposed to converted) are enemy Gliders. If you land on an enemy Glider or Base then you remove the entirety of the Glider you landed upon. When capturing a Glider from a Base, you are said to land on the enemy Glider's Core and you capture the Core with its respective Arms (note: you may choose one of two Gliders if you land on a Base with two stones of the Glider you are moving)

Moving off the edge: If a Glider cannot land safely before it reaches the board edge, then it is compelled to move off the board, destroying it.

Game End: You win a game of Gliss either by wiping all your opponent’s bases off the board (without eliminating all your own bases in the process in which case you lose) or if you start your turn with 12 of your own control towers on the board.

—description from the designer

In Back Whence Ye Came! mortal players cooperate in order to send the Devil back whence he came. With two distinct decks, one for the Devil called the "Devil Deck" and another for the mortal players called the "Mortal Deck", the mortal players draw cards that can be used to banish the cards that the Devil pulls from the "Devil Deck" to make his hand.

Each player has 12 "Soul Token"s that represent their fragile mortal souls. The Devil's Hand plays itself in an attempt to steal the "Soul Tokens" from the mortal players. The mortal players use the cards they draw from the "Mortal Deck" to banish the cards in the Devil's Hand, as well as grant themselves extra bonuses and abilities.

The Devil wins when the mortal players lose all of their "Soul Tokens". The players win once the Devil is left with no more cards in his hand. The game is balanced for 1-3 players, but you could theoretically play with more than 3. Yes, you read that right, you can even play the game solo.

There are also advanced rules that involve the mortal players having to protect their sanity as well as their souls. These rules are included in the core game deck and not in any expansion or anything like that. Can you protect your soul and sanity in the face of the horrors from beyond the veil?

Wiki Histories are quick little games that engage students in tough problem-solving. Each game is a snapshot of human history, following the same basic rules but adding a unique twist for variety.

Wiki Histories are not at the fun level of the best games you will find on boardgamegeek.com but that would be missing the point. The real strength of these games is that they are great for teaching problem-solving, history and board game design.

Teaching Board Game Design

Many schools in North America teach board game design, but students are rarely given enough constraints, so most of their game creations are too big and take too long to get played. The solution is for teachers to provide a template game that allows plenty of room for creativity within tight constraints. Wiki Histories take 15 minutes to play—short enough to be played in class. As pencil-and-paper games, they are also inexpensive for the budget-conscious classroom.

Most importantly, Wiki Histories offer students a realistic path to publication. After the first books are released, new books will be compiled from submitted designs. Students and their work will be celebrated. That will be inspiring for your students!

Teaching History

Playing games is one way to discover history. The simple mechanics of Wiki Histories do not seriously attempt to simulate history... however, the accompanying maps and mini-essays highlight little bits of history in a way that sticks with students. The mini-essays are written for high-schoolers, but the content can be simplified for younger students.

Teaching Problem-Solving

The #1 purpose of the elementary school math classroom is to get students to problem-solve. Start by splitting your class into two teams and projecting a Wiki History that only has a single map. Get the whole class to play. That’s why the territories have letters. Students can be called upon one at a time to claim a territory or attack a territory by just saying its letter.

An unexpected benefit of these games is that most of the maps come with extra information that students should disregard. Students need to filter information—distinguishing vital stuff from the background fluff.

A fun game that develops perception for two to four players from 3 to 12 years old can be easily played anywhere the child has to guess by feeling as many objects as possible. The game has three different ways of playing. The most common one is for the players to guess what is the object they are holding. The player who guesses the most is the winner. There are two more ways to play by using the empty punchboards of the game.
